one_line_takeaway: VeriGuard bolts a formally-verified, correct-by-construction policy function (proven with the Nagini verifier) onto an LLM agent as a runtime monitor, cutting Agent Security Bench attack success to 0% across four attack types while keeping the best task-success trade-off.
key_finding: On the ASB benchmark VeriGuard drives Attack Success Rate to exactly 0.0% across all four attack types (DPI/IPI/MP/PoT) for every backbone LLM tested, while simultaneously achieving the highest Task Success Rate among all defenses (best security-vs-utility trade-off). On access-control benchmarks it reaches perfect 100% accuracy/precision/recall on EICU-AC and the best recall (100% with GPT-4.1) on Mind2Web-SC, notable because it is a generic policy constructor with no in-context learning, unlike the task-tailored GuardAgent baseline. The paper argues recall is the most critical security metric (block every violation) even at some precision cost. The ablation shows the biggest single ASR drop comes from the policy-generation step (53.5% -> ~9.97%), with validation completing the reduction to 0%.
core_contribution: VeriGuard is a framework that gives LLM-based agents formal, provable safety guarantees by moving safety enforcement from reactive filtering to 'correct-by-construction' verified code. Its own contribution is a dual-stage architecture: (1) an expensive OFFLINE policy-generation stage where an LLM synthesizes a Python 'policy function' plus a set of formal pre/post-condition constraints from a natural-language security request and an agent specification, then iteratively refines them through a three-step loop (validation/disambiguation, PyTest-based unit testing, and formal verification via the Nagini program verifier proving the Hoare triple {C_pre} p {C_post}); and (2) a lightweight ONLINE stage where the pre-verified policy acts as a runtime monitor that intercepts each proposed agent action and allows/denies it before execution. A secondary contribution is the empirical comparison of four enforcement strategies on how to intervene when a violation is detected. Note: guard-agent approaches like GuardAgent and ShieldAgent are cited as prior/related work, not this paper's contribution; VeriGuard distinguishes itself by using formal verification rather than empirical/reactive reasoning.
threat_model: Autonomous tool-using LLM agents deployed in sensitive domains (healthcare, finance, cloud infra, autonomous driving) that may deviate from user objectives, violate data-handling policies, or be actively compromised. The paper adopts the Agent Security Bench threat taxonomy of four adversarial attacks: direct prompt injection (DPI), indirect prompt injection (IPI), memory poisoning (MP), and plan-of-thought (PoT) backdoors, plus access-control violations where an agent must deny actions forbidden for a given user role. Attackers are assumed capable of crafting novel adversarial inputs that bypass static pattern-matching guardrails; VeriGuard's defense is a verified policy that intercepts actions at enforcement points before execution.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) soundness of the formal verification is only as good as the LLM-generated constraints, which are non-deterministic and error-prone, so it ultimately depends on manual user validation that the constraints capture true intent; (2) capabilities are bounded by the Nagini verifier, which has a limited grammar for complex properties and is itself an active research project; (3) extending beyond Python to other languages is a non-trivial implementation challenge; (4) the hybrid LLM-argument-extraction + deterministic-Python-rule design may miss sophisticated attacks needing deeper logical reasoning or dynamic policy updates. Reviewer-noted: the offline verification stage is computationally heavy, and the online argument-extraction step still relies on an LLM (read_parameters_with_LLM), which is a non-verified component in the runtime path.

datasets_used: Agent Security Bench (ASB) - 10 ReAct agents, 10 scenarios, 10 tasks, 13 attack variations, 400 attack tools; attacks: DPI, IPI, memory poisoning, PoT backdoor
EICU-AC - ICU QA agent access control over 10 patient databases with role-restricted data categories
Mind2Web-SC - safety-control variant of Mind2Web web-agent benchmark (2000+ tasks) with user-based prohibition rules
quantitative_results: ASB: VeriGuard ASR = 0.0% on DPI/IPI/MP/PoT for all backbones (Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4.1, Claude-Sonnet-4)
EICU-AC: VeriGuard = 100.0 accuracy / 100.0 precision / 100.0 recall for GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, and Gemini-2.5-pro
Mind2Web-SC: VeriGuard (Gemini-2.5-pro) = 97.2 acc / 95.0 P / 99.0 R; VeriGuard (GPT-4.1) = 96.2 / 91.2 / 100.0 R; VeriGuard (GPT-4o) = 95.1 / 91.3 / 99.0
Ablation (Gemini-2.5-Flash): initial avg ASR 53.5% -> Policy Generation ~9.97% -> Validation 0% across all attack vectors; TSR rises 51.87% -> 63.25%

Enforcement strategies (ASB, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Table 3): Task Termination 0.0 ASR / 0.0 TSR; Action Blocking 0.0 / 38.1; Tool Execution Halt 0.0 / 44.6; Collaborative Re-planning 11.9 ASR / 62.1 TSR; CRP+TEH hybrid 0.0 ASR / 63.3 TSR (text reports optimal 0.1% ASR / 63.6% TSR)

GuardRail LLM baseline also reaches 0.0 ASR on ASB but with much lower TSR (e.g. 40.2 vs VeriGuard 63.3 on Gemini-Flash), showing detection is easy but precise low-utility intervention is the hard part
baselines_compared: ASB: No Attack (upper bound), No Defense (lower bound), Delimiter, Paraphrasing, Dynamic Prompt (DP) Rewriting, and a stronger GuardRail LLM-judge baseline
EICU-AC / Mind2Web-SC: Hardcoded Safety Rules, LLaMA-Guard 3, AgentMonitor (GPT-4o), GuardAgent (GPT-4), AGrail (GPT-4o) - values taken from cited papers

Notable baseline results: GuardAgent EICU-AC 98.7/100/97.5 and Mind2Web-SC 90.0/100/80.0; AGrail Mind2Web-SC 98.4 acc / 99.0 P / 98.0 R (better accuracy/precision than VeriGuard there); backbone LLMs evaluated: Gemini 2.5 Flash & Pro, GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, Claude-Sonnet-4
